The surgical team places trocars under twelve millimeter pressure, ensuring careful insertion to avoid bowel loops. The assistant is unable to manipulate the uterus due to table obstruction, necessitating the use of either three five-millimeter trocars or a ten-millimeter trocar in the middle to facilitate vaginal suturing.

The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity for adhesions, freeing them before inserting trocars. The cecum and ileum are gently pushed aside to examine the appendix, confirming its absence due to a previous appendectomy. The bowel is carefully observed for solitary nodules of endometriosis, which could potentially cause occlusion post-surgery.

The surgeon examines the adnexa and the Douglas pouch, noting the patient's previous caesarean section. Using bipolar scissors or the Thunderbeat device, the surgeon begins the hysterectomy by asking the assistant to position the uterus laterally and in an intermediate position to expose the round ligament. The round ligament is grasped, coagulated, and cut. The assistant holds the round ligament to make the anterior leaf of the broad ligament accessible, which is then incised towards the isthmus.

The surgeon instructs the assistant to push the uterus and valve into the vagina to expose the vaginal cul-de-sac. The peritoneum of the bladder is incised, and visible vessels are coagulated individually. A window is created in the broad ligament above the ureter, ensuring the ureter remains below.

The surgeon holds the fallopian tube and uses bipolar energy to coagulate and cut along its limit, ensuring the ovary remains vascularized. The fallopian tubes are removed to reduce the risk of ovarian cancer, while the ovaries are preserved.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there is no bleeding. They then take the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and begin dissection, pushing the ureter laterally to avoid injury. The dissection is directed toward the root of the uterosacral ligament, ensuring the ureter is identified and preserved.

The surgeon coagulates and incises the uterine vessels at the level of the isthmus, ensuring the ureter is safely positioned below. The round ligament is incised on the opposite side, with the uterus in an intermediate position. The valve is pushed to visualize the vaginal cul-de-sac, and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is incised toward the isthmus. A window is created, revealing the thin black part of the leaf. The fallopian tube is held, showing interruption due to previous tubal ligation.
The surgeon checks for perfect hemostasis in the surgical field, ensuring no bleeding is present. The valve is pushed to maintain exposure of the surgical area.

The surgeon pushes the valve laterally to displace the ureter, enhancing the safety of the dissection. The uterus is anteverted to work on the posterior leaf towards the uterosacral ligament. The assistant holds the round ligament while the surgeon uses a bipolar device, ensuring the ureter is safely positioned away from the dissection area. The surgeon waits for the disappearance of bubbles, indicating complete tissue coagulation, before proceeding to cut.

The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and uses a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis. The assistant holds the round ligament while the valve is pushed laterally to pull the ureter away from the surgical field. Bleeding is observed from an opened vein, and the ureter is visible nearby.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is visible near the site of bleeding. The bleeding appears to originate from a vein opened tangentially, close to the ureter. The ureter is confirmed to be in view, and its movement is observed to ensure its position.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to grasp a vessel, ensuring the ureter is visible and safely positioned away from the surgical field.

Maria pushes the valve to displace the ureter, allowing the surgeon to control the uterine pedicles at the isthmus. The uterine artery is visible, and the surgeon cuts the uterine pedicles, leading to the devascularization of the uterus. The uterus changes color due to the loss of blood supply, and bleeding from the uterus is expected to stop spontaneously.

Maria pushes the valve anteriorly to dissect the bladder, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The surgeon incises the cervix and vagina, maintaining contact with the cervix. The bladder is identified and pushed to achieve a good edge for closing the vagina.

The surgeon opens the vaginal wall using a monopolar hook or energy device, creating an incision in close contact with the cervix. The third operator turns the valve counterclockwise from the left side of the patient, facilitating the incision. The assistant holds the round ligament as the pneumoperitoneum is lost upon opening the vagina.

The surgeon works on the left side, holding the round ligament while the third operator gently pushes to expose the surgical area. The vagina is opened, and care is taken to avoid contact with the bowel when using a monopolar hook to prevent fistula formation. The assistant holds the endoscope securely.

The surgeon positions the valve in front and lowers the uterus, preparing to incise over the right cul-de-sac. Care is taken not to push the uterus too much to avoid breaking the vagina. The incision is made in contact with the cervix, avoiding lateral cuts from previous coagulation. The valve separates the structures, increasing the distance between the instrument and the ureter, which is pushed laterally.

The assistant gently removes the uterus through the vaginal opening, completing the hysterectomy procedure. The valve is retracted to expose the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structures.
